title: Missing Unicode blocks
author: darealshinji
body:
It looks like many of the additional Latin and Cyrillic sets are missing right now?
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Category:Latin-script_Unicode_blocks
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Category:Cyrillic_Unicode_blocks

There's also Armenian and Cherokee that have upper and lower variants.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cherokee_(Unicode_block)
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cherokee_Supplement

Cherokee upper to lower:
U+13A0 - U+13EF -> U+AB70 - U+ABBF
U+13F0 - U+13F5 -> U+13F8 - U+13FD

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Armenian_(Unicode_block)
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Armenian_alphabet#Alphabet

Armenian upper to lower:
U+0531 - U+0556 -> U+0561 - U+0586

title: utf8casestr is unsafe regarding length if input strings and should have a variant with size limits
author: mgexo

title: [Documentation] taglib needs a utf8.h file - is this file here the right one?
author: rubyFeedback
body:
Today I wanted to compile a fresh git clone checkout from taglib:

    /home/x/Temp/rbt/taglib-08.07.2024/taglib/toolkit/tstring.cpp:31:10: fatal error: utf8.h: No such file or directory
     31 | #include <utf8.h>
     | ^~~~~~~~
    compilation terminated.

taglib wants some header called utf8.h.

Is the project here the right one?

Would it be possible to mention this on the main README?

Right now I am not entirely certain how utf8.h plays into this. Perhaps it is a totally unrelated project,
I really don't know. I did not write any of the code so I am not sure if I need this utf8.h or not.

Could the main README explain the use cases of utf8.h here on a modern linux system? In particular
if you know whether some projects need it or not. taglib is not the only one that suddenly asks for
utf8.h. At first I thought this comes from glibc, but now I am not sure, which is why any improvement
to the main README would be so useful, to know whether I am on the wrong track. (My binutils is
acting weirdly too, almost as if it assumes I use BSD rather than linux; not sure if this is involved here
or not.)

title: grapheme support

title: utf8makevalid : test to identify sequence length and possible values not sufficient
author: JPDelprat
body:
Hello,

In utf8makevalid, you use the following test to identify a 4 sequence bytes

"if (0xf0 == (0xf8 & *read))"

This is not correct if you suppose that you can have any invalid string as an input parameter, since only a few values in f0-ff ranges are valid. 

Moreover, for valid values in f0-ff  ranges, possible values for second byte are not the same one. For example, with f0, valid range for second byte is 90..bf, instead of 80..bf

Regards

title: utf8upr/lwr size issues?
author: ghost
body:
Hi, I was looking at the docs for `utf8upr`/`lwr`, and they don't seem to indicate what happens if the string passed to them doesn't have enough space for the new codepoints. I understand that letters may have different byte sizes in their upper/lowercase variants, so I was wondering whether `utf8upr`/`lwr` will allocate extra memory as required.

Looking at the code, though, it seems like they just call `utf8catcodepoint`, which AFAIK doesn't allocate additional memory. In fact, the `size` argument in that call is set to the size of the new codepoint, rather than the size of the buffer as it should be. Is this correct?

title: strn*/utf8n* functions
author: crt333
body:
The utf8len function returns codepoints instead of bytes, as expected, but it seems things like utf8ncmp continue to use bytes, which wasn't what I expected. Perhaps utf8ncmp could use n in codepoints too, and another utf8bcmp could use b bytes? 

Not at all critical, since a work-around is easy, and I have no idea if others would want codepoint counting instead of bytes for the n functions. I needed an n codepoint compare, so I noticed this.

title: `utf8nvalid` reads out bounds

title: Possibility of dual-licensing?
author: thomasfsteeples
body:
utf8.h is currently published under the [Unlicense](https://unlicense.org/), putting its work in the public domain. This is great, but there are [open questions](
 https://softwareengineering.stackexchange.com/questions/147111/what-is-wrong-with-the-unlicense) as to whether this is valid in all jurisdictions (Germany being the most famous example).

As such, would you be at all willing to consider dual-licensing this software under the Unlicense and another "fallback" license? The [CC0 license](https://creativecommons.org/share-your-work/public-domain/cc0/) is another public domain license with a clause for what should happen when the terms of the license are deemed invalid under local law. Alternatively, there exist other minimal OSI approved licenses (such as the MIT license, the ISC license and the BSD licenses) which are permissive. These typically require attribution from the user, but if the software were dual-licensed, it would be entirely their choice which license they want to use.

Absolutely no worries if this is too big an ask, just really want to be able to use this software in a more legally-watertight way.

title: clang-format?
author: Guekka
body:
While working on #92, I noticed there was no clang format file provided with utf8.h
I tried to respect the formatting, but it's hard to be always consistent
Providing a clang format file would allow contributors to follow the repo style guide easily

title: Support constexpr?
author: Guekka
body:
Hey, thanks for this library. I'd like to be able to use it in constexpr context.
Would be willing to add a `utf8_constexpr` macro, that would be used in contexts that would allow it?
I might be able to work on a PR, but I don't have a time estimate
